1: The invitation to all who are thirsty opens without qualification — not all of Israel but all who recognize their need. Come to the waters; come, buy and eat; buy wine and milk without money and without price. The economic paradox — buy without money — strips away the normal mechanism of exchange and declares that what YHWH offers is beyond market logic. Thirst and hunger are the condition of exile; the invitation offers exactly what exile cannot provide. The whole nefesh (soul, disp 3) — the whole self — is what thirsts, and the whole self is what YHWH feeds. Jesus draws on this imagery in John 7:37 and Revelation 22:17.
2: Why do you spend money on what is not bread, and your wages on what does not satisfy? The question diagnoses the exile's futile labor: working for Babylon, acquiring what cannot feed the soul. Listen carefully to me and eat what is good; let your nefesh (soul, disp 3) delight in richness. The alternative to dissatisfying toil is to incline the ear to YHWH — hearing is the mode of access to the banquet. This is the economics of grace: the satisfying food is received by attention, not by labor.
3: Incline your ear and come to me; hear, and you will live; and I will make an everlasting covenant (berit olam, berit disp 3, olam disp 3) with you — my reliable hesed (covenant love, disp 4) promised to David. The Davidic covenant — the unconditional promise of 2 Sam 7 — is the model and the content of what YHWH now extends to the whole community. The individual king's covenant is democratized: all who hear and come participate in the Davidic promises. Berit olam and hesed David are the two deepest covenant terms, stacked together to name what YHWH is offering.
4: David was made a witness, leader, and commander for the peoples — his role was never merely national. The Davidic covenant always had a universal scope (Ps 2, 72). By democratizing the Davidic covenant (v 3), YHWH is extending the universal witness-function to the whole restored community.
5: You will summon a nation you did not know, and a nation that did not know you will run to you, because of the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel. The expansion echoes 54:3 and the Abrahamic promise: Zion becomes the center to which nations stream. The motivation is entirely YHWH's initiative — the nations are drawn not by Israel's attractiveness but by YHWH's glory revealed through Israel.
6: Seek the LORD while he may be found; call on him while he is near. The urgency is temporal — there is a window. This is not simply a timeless invitation but a kairos-moment call. The exilic community stands at the turning point when YHWH is near in the sense that the announcement of return (Isaiah 52:7-12) has been made; delay means missing the moment of return.
7: Let the wicked forsake his way and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return to the LORD, who will have mercy (rahem — womb-compassion), and to our God, who will abundantly pardon. The return YHWH requires is not first geographic (return from Babylon) but moral (forsake wickedness). The pardon is abundant — yarbeh lisloak, literally will multiply to pardon. YHWH's forgiveness is not reluctant or minimal but extravagant. The double mercy (rahem and pardon) grounds the call to return.
8: For my thoughts are not your thoughts, and your ways are not my ways. This is the foundation of the abundant-pardon verse: YHWH pardons more than humans think possible because he thinks and acts on a different plane. The for connects the limitless pardon (v 7) to the limitless otherness of YHWH's mind (vv 8-9). This is also the ground for the sovereign initiative of vv 1-6: YHWH invites, acts, and accomplishes in ways that transcend human calculation.
9: As the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts higher than your thoughts. The spatial image gives scale to the qualitative difference. This is not skepticism about human reason but an assertion that YHWH's thoughts and ways belong to a different order of magnitude. The invitation to seek the LORD (v 6) is therefore both urgent and humble: we seek one whose ways we cannot anticipate or control.
10: The word of YHWH is compared to rain and snow that come down from heaven — they do not return until they have accomplished their purpose: watered the earth, made it sprout, given seed and bread. The hydrological cycle becomes a metaphor for the efficacy of divine speech: just as precipitation achieves its agricultural purpose without fail, so YHWH's word achieves its purpose without fail.
11: So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth; it shall not return to me empty, but it shall accomplish what I purpose and succeed in the thing for which I sent it. The word sent here is the entire promise-complex of chapters 40-55: comfort, return, Servant, covenant. This verse is the retroactive authorization of everything proclaimed in these chapters. The word does not merely describe what will happen; it is itself the agent that brings it about. John 1:1-3 and Hebrews 1:2-3 inhabit the logic of this verse in their identification of the eternal Word as the agent of creation and redemption.
12: For you will go out with joy and be led forth in shalom (peace, disp 2). The exodus-language: go out (yetse'u) and be led forth (tuvalun) are exodus verbs. But the second exodus is marked by joy and shalom rather than haste and fear (Isaiah 52:12). The mountains and hills shall burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands — creation joins the processional, extending the singing-ruins of 52:9 to the entire natural world. The new exodus is a cosmic event, not merely a political one.
13: Instead of the thorn the cypress shall come up, and instead of the brier the myrtle shall come up. The transformation of the wilderness through which the exiles return is a sign (ot) and an everlasting memorial (olam, disp 3) that will not be cut off. The sign function of the transformed landscape: creation's changed state is a standing testimony to what YHWH has done. The covenant of shalom (Isaiah 54:10) and the everlasting covenant (Isaiah 55:3) are written in the renewed creation as well as in the community's life.
